quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Net change theorem"?

options.text: f⁻¹ reverses f: if f(a) = b, then f⁻¹(b) = a; domain of f⁻¹ = range of f, and vice versa
∫ₐᵇ f'(x)dx = f(b) - f(a); the integral of a rate gives the net change in the quantity
Find dy/dt given dx/dt (or vice versa) by differentiating an equation relating x and y with respect to time t
State the integral being evaluated, show the antiderivative, substitute limits, and simplify; credit for setup even if computation errors occur

quiz_questions.explanation: "Net change theorem" is defined as: ∫ₐᵇ f'(x)dx = f(b) - f(a); the integral of a rate gives the net change in the quantity. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Accumulation interpretation"?

options.text: State the theorem precisely, verify hypotheses, apply the conclusion, and interpret in the context of the problem
From a graph, check if the function approaches the same value from the left and right; if not, the two-sided limit does not exist
∫ₐˣ f'(t)dt represents the accumulated change from time a to time x; add to initial value for total: f(x) = f(a) + ∫ₐˣ f'(t)dt
The + C in every indefinite integral accounts for the fact that F(x) + C has the same derivative for any constant C

quiz_questions.explanation: "Accumulation interpretation" is defined as: ∫ₐˣ f'(t)dt represents the accumulated change from time a to time x; add to initial value for total: f(x) = f(a) + ∫ₐˣ f'(t)dt. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Total vs. net"?

options.text: d/dx[f(x) ± g(x)] = f'(x) ± g'(x); derivative of a sum/difference is the sum/difference of derivatives
The tolerance for how close f(x) must be to L; represents the desired precision of the output
A relation where y is defined implicitly by an equation F(x,y) = 0 rather than explicitly as y = f(x)
Net change: ∫f'(t)dt (can cancel positive and negative); total change: ∫|f'(t)|dt (always adds up)

quiz_questions.explanation: "Total vs. net" is defined as: Net change: ∫f'(t)dt (can cancel positive and negative); total change: ∫|f'(t)|dt (always adds up). The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Rate in minus rate out"?

options.text: f''(x) > 0; the graph curves upward like a cup; the slope is increasing
Net accumulation = ∫(rate in - rate out)dt; used for tank, population, and economic problems
If f'(x) = 0 for all x in an interval, MVT proves f must be a constant function on that interval
Priority for choosing u: Logarithmic > Inverse trig > Algebraic > Trigonometric > Exponential; u is differentiated, dv is integrated

quiz_questions.explanation: "Rate in minus rate out" is defined as: Net accumulation = ∫(rate in - rate out)dt; used for tank, population, and economic problems. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Units of the integral"?

options.text: Left: sample at left endpoint; Right: sample at right endpoint; Midpoint: sample at center of each subinterval
First derivative test: always works but requires sign analysis on an interval; second derivative test: simpler but can be inconclusive
The derivative of the natural log; note that d/dx[ln|x|] = 1/x for x ≠ 0
Units of ∫f'(t)dt = [units of f'(t)] × [units of t] = units of f(t)

quiz_questions.explanation: "Units of the integral" is defined as: Units of ∫f'(t)dt = [units of f'(t)] × [units of t] = units of f(t). The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "∫ₐᵇ f'(x)dx = f(b) - f(a); the integral of a rate gives the net change in the quantity"?

options.text: When does the particle change direction?
Net change theorem
Linear approximation
d/dx[ln x] = 1/x

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Net change theorem".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "∫ₐˣ f'(t)dt represents the accumulated change from time a to time x; add to initial value for total: f(x) = f(a) + ∫ₐˣ f'(t)dt"?

options.text: Angular rate problems
d/dx[cos x] = -sin x
Accumulation interpretation
Lorenz curve

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Accumulation interpretation".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "Net change: ∫f'(t)dt (can cancel positive and negative); total change: ∫|f'(t)|dt (always adds up)"?

options.text: Removable discontinuity
Total vs. net
Derivative of tan x
Table-based accumulation

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Total vs. net".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "Net accumulation = ∫(rate in - rate out)dt; used for tank, population, and economic problems"?

options.text: Displacement vs. distance
Equilibrium solutions
Rate in minus rate out
d/dx[f(x)^g(x)]

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Rate in minus rate out".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "Units of ∫f'(t)dt = [units of f'(t)] × [units of t] = units of f(t)"?

options.text: Integration by parts indicators
Units of the integral
Multiple chain rule
Shadow problems

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Units of the integral".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following statements about Net Change and Accumulation is accurate?

options.text: Net Change and Accumulation applies the Fundamental Theorem in context: ∫ₐᵇ f'(x)dx = f(b) - f(a), so the integral of a rate of change over [a,b] equals the net change.
Basic antiderivative rules: ∫xⁿdx = xⁿ⁺¹/(n+1) + C (n ≠ -1), ∫eˣdx = eˣ + C, ∫(1/x)dx = ln|x| + C, ∫cos x dx = sin x + C, ∫sin x dx = -cos x + C.
This technique is essential for functions like xˣ, (sin x)^x, or x^(1/x) where both base and exponent are variable.
Continuous compound interest follows A = Pe^(rt).

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Net Change and Accumulation as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which statement correctly describes an aspect of Net Change and Accumulation?

options.text: If f'(t) is the rate of water flowing into a tank, then ∫₀ᵀ f'(t)dt is the net change in water volume.
The Mean Value Theorem for Integrals guarantees there exists a c in [a,b] where f(c) = f_avg—the function actually achieves its average value at some point.
Basic antiderivative rules: ∫xⁿdx = xⁿ⁺¹/(n+1) + C (n ≠ -1), ∫eˣdx = eˣ + C, ∫(1/x)dx = ln|x| + C, ∫cos x dx = sin x + C, ∫sin x dx = -cos x + C.
Second Derivative Test provides a quick classification of critical points using concavity.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Net Change and Accumulation as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which is true specifically about Net Change and Accumulation?

options.text: The sign of the derivative indicates whether the quantity is increasing or decreasing, and the second derivative reveals acceleration or deceleration of change.
Basic Derivative Rules provide shortcuts for finding derivatives without using the limit definition every time.
Net change can be positive or negative.
Continuous compound interest follows A = Pe^(rt).

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Net Change and Accumulation as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best explains a key idea of Net Change and Accumulation?

options.text: Riemann sums approximate this area using rectangles: left, right, and midpoint sums with n subintervals.
To find the total accumulated quantity when rate can be negative, integrate the absolute value: ∫|f'(t)|dt gives total variation.
A Riemann sum divides [a,b] into n subintervals and approximates the area using rectangles: Σᵢf(xᵢ*)Δx.
After integrating in terms of u, substitute back: u = g(x).

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Net Change and Accumulation as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which accurately reflects the content on Net Change and Accumulation?

options.text: Formal Definition of Limits (Epsilon-Delta) provides the rigorous mathematical foundation for limits.
Net Change and Accumulation applies the Fundamental Theorem in context: ∫ₐᵇ f'(x)dx = f(b) - f(a), so the integral of a rate of change over [a,b] equals the net change.
The solution is P(t) = K/(1 + Ae^(-rt)) where A = (K-P₀)/P₀.
Antiderivatives: Inverse Trigonometric Forms produces inverse trig functions from specific integrand patterns.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Net Change and Accumulation as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following is a real-world application of Net Change and Accumulation?

options.text: Traffic flow: ∫₇ᵃᵐ⁹ᵃᵐ (cars entering - cars leaving)dt gives the net change in cars on a highway during morning rush hour
AP exam problems frequently ask students to apply MVT to piecewise data: 'Given the table, must there be a value c where f'(c) = 3?'
Acceleration as the second derivative of position: a car speeding up has positive s''(t), slowing down has negative s''(t), and constant speed has s''(t) = 0
Radiocarbon dating: ¹⁴C decays with t₁/₂ ≈ 5,730 years; measuring remaining ¹⁴C in organic material determines its age up to ~50,000 years

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Net Change and Accumulation.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which example demonstrates the practical relevance of Net Change and Accumulation?

options.text: Economics: ∫₀ᵀ (revenue rate - cost rate)dt = total profit over the period [0,T]
Engineering: computing cross-sectional area of unusually shaped beams or channels for structural and flow analysis
Population growth modeling: d/dt[P(t)] where P involves power functions helps ecologists predict changes in wildlife populations
Compound interest: computing d/dn[(1 + r/n)^(nt)] using logarithmic differentiation to analyze how compounding frequency affects growth

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Net Change and Accumulation.

quiz_questions.question_text: How is Net Change and Accumulation applied in real-world contexts?

options.text: Medical imaging: CT scans reconstruct 3D images by computing rates of change of X-ray attenuation through tissue from multiple angles
Hydrology: ∫(inflow rate - outflow rate)dt gives the net change in reservoir water level over a drought period
Minimizing travel time: finding the optimal route combining different speed zones (e.g., running on sand vs. swimming) using calculus of optimization
Mountain hiking: the highest point on a trail (absolute max) and the lowest point (absolute min) are found by checking peaks, valleys, and the trail endpoints

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Net Change and Accumulation.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which scenario illustrates the principles of Net Change and Accumulation?

options.text: Traffic flow: ∫₇ᵃᵐ⁹ᵃᵐ (cars entering - cars leaving)dt gives the net change in cars on a highway during morning rush hour
Computing moments of inertia in physics: ∫x²·f(x)dx often requires integration by parts, essential for structural and mechanical engineering
Algorithm analysis in computer science: O(n log n) sorting algorithms (merge sort) outperform O(n²) algorithms (bubble sort) for large datasets
Market saturation: adoption of new technology (smartphones, streaming services) follows an S-curve as the product approaches market saturation

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Net Change and Accumulation.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best shows Net Change and Accumulation in practice?

options.text: Economics: ∫₀ᵀ (revenue rate - cost rate)dt = total profit over the period [0,T]
Finding the slope of a circle x² + y² = 25 at any point: dy/dx = -x/y, giving the tangent line direction for navigation and engineering applications
Population modeling: P(t) = P₀e^(rt), so P'(t) = rP₀e^(rt) = rP(t), showing exponential growth rate is proportional to population size
Scientific measurement: using increasingly precise instruments to estimate a true value—smaller measurement intervals approximate the 'limit' of the quantity

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Net Change and Accumulation.
